title: Executive Order 8565—Amendment of Executive Order No. 8389 of April 10, 1940, as Amended

flags.justification: This extends 'all the provisions' of EO 8389 — including the broad Treasury Secretary licensing discretion previously identified in that lineage (see EO 8785, EO 9989) — to a new country, Romania.
Inherits the parent framework's absence of independent review for affected persons or property.
Single, narrow purpose: extending an existing framework to one additional country.
Inherits the elastic 'national' and interest-of-any-nature definitional structure previously identified in this lineage.
No incentive structure distinct from the vagueness already captured under Flag 4.
Inherits the parent framework's lack of an independent review or expiration mechanism tied to the underlying wartime property-control emergency.
This extension does not itself remove any additional oversight beyond what the base framework already established.
Inherits the parent framework's lack of an independent check on the Treasury Secretary's licensing discretion.
A routine, formulaic extension of an already-established framework to one additional country, not itself a novel authority claim.
No second agency's competing mandate is implicated.
No new exemption structure is created by this extension.

notes: Validation batch (negative). Structural weight: 5/18 = 27.8%. Methodologically notable: this one-paragraph order simply extends the Trading with the Enemy Act / IEEPA-predecessor framework of EO 8389 to Romania, and inherits that framework's structural properties rather than creating anything new. A negative-class label reflects that Mayer & Price did not judge this specific extension order independently historically significant — it does not mean the order is structurally weightless. Structural weight can be inherited through incorporation by reference, and this is a clean illustration of that dynamic, distinct from the earlier finding for EO 9989 (a routine transfer within the same lineage) and worth keeping in mind when interpreting any negative-class EOs that turn out to score above the floor.
